metadata.summary: Interactive product representation before development. Choose fidelity (low/medium/high) by learning needs: concept → paper, flow → clicks, experience → high-fidelity.

content.title: Prototyping
content.summary: A prototype is an interactive representation of a product that simulates the user experience before development. Choose fidelity (low/medium/high) based on what you need to learn — concept validation needs paper, flow testing needs clicks, experience testing needs high-fidelity. Always define learning objectives before choosing a tool.

why.p: Static designs cannot convey interactions. Usability issues stay hidden until development, when fixing them is expensive. Prototyping surfaces interaction gaps with real users before a single line of production code is written.

list.item: Validating whether users can complete a critical flow before development starts.
Aligning stakeholders on how something will work, not just how it looks.
Testing a risky design assumption (navigation structure, onboarding, checkout).
Deciding fidelity level given project constraints and open questions.
Generating a structured prototype plan and usability test script from a design brief.
The interaction is motion-dependent or too nuanced for text-based planning (agent can plan, not build).
A live coded prototype is required — that crosses from UX planning into implementation.
No clear testing hypothesis exists — prototyping without defined learning goals wastes cycles.
Post-launch optimization where A/B testing or analytics provide faster signal.

section.name: fidelity-decision-matrix

rule.statement: Choose fidelity based on what you need to learn, not on how much time you have.
rule.rationale: Fidelity that exceeds learning needs wastes design time and creates false attachment to visuals before validation.

section.p: Low fidelity (paper, basic wireframes) → concept testing, hours to create. Medium fidelity (clickable screens, key interactions) → flow testing, days to create. High fidelity (near-final, animated) → experience testing, weeks to create. Code prototype (HTML/CSS/React) → technical feasibility, engineering time.

section.name: five-step-process

rule.statement: Write learning objectives before selecting tools or fidelity level.
rule.rationale: Objectives determine scope; scope determines fidelity; fidelity determines tool. Reversing this order produces over-built prototypes.

section.p: Step 1: Define objectives — state each as a testable question (e.g., "Can users complete checkout without help?"). Step 2: Determine fidelity — use the matrix above. When in doubt, start lower. Step 3: Create prototype — include key screens, critical interactions, realistic content, and a clear user path. Prototype the riskiest assumption first. Step 4: Test with users — define 3-5 tasks, observe without intervening, note failures and confusion. Step 5: Iterate — fix major issues, re-test if critical flows changed, document decisions, hand off.

rule.statement: Limit moderated test tasks to 3-5 per session.
rule.rationale: More tasks produce fatigue and diluted observations, not more insight.

section.name: prototype-types
section.p: Paper — sketches and paper screens; fast, cheap, anyone can facilitate. Clickable (Figma hotspots) — linked screens; quick to build, shareable, testable. Interactive (Figma/Framer/ProtoPie) — animations and transitions; realistic but time-intensive. Code (React/HTML) — technical validation; highest fidelity, real implementation constraints.

example.title: Clickable Figma prototype for checkout flow validation
example.p: Create screen designs, add hotspots, link screens, define transitions. Tests flow comprehension without investing in animations.

example.title: Building a ProtoPie interactive prototype with full animations before any user has seen the basic flow
example.p: Wastes days on polish that testing may invalidate entirely.

section.name: handoff-artifacts
section.p: When handing off to development, include: prototype link (interactive reference), screen specs (measurements), interaction specs (behavior definitions), assets (icons/images), component library reference, and documented edge cases (error states, empty states).

rule.statement: Document what was NOT prototyped and why.
rule.rationale: Prevents stakeholder scope creep during handoff; sets correct expectations for what remains unvalidated.

rule.statement: Prototype only the flows that answer your stated learning objectives.
rule.rationale: Prototyping everything wastes time and makes tests too long. Focused scope produces actionable findings.

rule.statement: Use realistic content — real product names and plausible data — not Lorem Ipsum.
rule.rationale: Placeholder text invalidates navigation and reading behavior; users cannot evaluate information hierarchy with dummy content.

antipattern.description: Too high fidelity too early — polishing visuals before flow validation.
antipattern.reason: Wastes design time and creates emotional attachment to visuals that testing may invalidate.

antipattern.description: Building a prototype but not testing it with users.
antipattern.reason: The artifact has no value without learning; testing is the point.

antipattern.description: Ignoring test findings — iterating the design without addressing discovered issues.
antipattern.reason: Testing without acting on results is a waste of participant time and produces no product improvement.

antipattern.description: No clear hypothesis — prototyping without defined questions.
antipattern.reason: Without a hypothesis, findings cannot be evaluated and the next iteration has no direction.

rule.statement: Agent-generated test scripts must be reviewed for leading questions before use with participants.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to suggestive phrasing ("did you find it easy to..."); neutral questions are required for valid qualitative data.

rule.statement: A single participant who cannot complete a critical task is a severity-1 finding, regardless of sample size.
rule.rationale: Minority findings must not be averaged away; severity is determined by impact, not frequency alone.

rule.statement: The agent must not decide unilaterally to proceed to development; that decision belongs to the designer and PM after reviewing synthesis.
rule.rationale: Agent synthesis is advisory; the ship/hold decision requires human judgment on risk appetite and product context.

rule.statement: Code prototypes written by an agent are not production code; do not commit to main or use as implementation basis without full engineering review.
rule.rationale: Agent HTML/CSS prototypes miss micro-interaction nuance and may use patterns incompatible with the production stack.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent receives a feature brief (user goal, technical constraints, open design questions) and outputs a complete Prototype Plan document: objectives, fidelity rationale, scope (included/excluded flows), interactive element table, and a usability test script with tasks. After human-run testing, a second agent pass synthesizes raw session notes into a ranked issue list with severity ratings and recommended design changes.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Playwright: Automate interaction with HTML/code prototypes for smoke-test validation (npm install -D playwright, https://playwright.dev). Puppeteer: Screenshot HTML prototypes at multiple viewport sizes (npm install puppeteer, https://pptr.dev). http-server: Serve static HTML prototype locally without build step (npm install -g http-server, https://github.com/http-party/http-server). Storybook: Component-level interactive prototyping in code (React, Angular, Vue) (npx storybook@latest init, https://storybook.js.org).

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Figma (SaaS): Read-only API can export frames/components; prototype creation requires manual UI. Maze (SaaS): Unmoderated test platform; create tests via UI, export results as CSV for agent synthesis. UserTesting (SaaS): Session recordings exported manually; transcripts can be fed to agent for synthesis. ProtoPie Connect (SaaS/desktop): Advanced animations; no scriptable API. Framer (SaaS): Code components (React) embeddable; agent can write component code. Loom (SaaS): Useful for async stakeholder reviews of prototype walkthroughs.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Always write learning objectives before choosing fidelity — fidelity follows what you need to learn, not timeline pressure. Prototype the riskiest assumption in the design, not the easiest-to-build flow. Use realistic content (real product names, plausible data) — placeholder text invalidates navigation and reading behavior tests. Limit moderated test tasks to 3-5 per session; more tasks produce fatigue, not more insight. Document "what we did NOT prototype and why" — this prevents stakeholder scope creep during handoff. After testing, produce a diff of the pre-test prototype plan vs. post-test findings before starting iteration.

template.purpose: Prototype plan: objectives, fidelity, scope, interactive elements, test script
Per-session observation log: tasks, quotes, issues, recommendations
Bash script to scaffold a minimal HTML click-through prototype
